### c-mo1uw0ah-06b417bbd201 [concluded]
> Should specs prescribe implementation or describe intent and constraints?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Intent and constraints. The planner's job is decision closure and context distillation, not predicting code. Overly prescriptive specs suppress implementer judgment and propagate planner errors faithfully. Validated against w-mny1zoc9 — its prescriptive rename table missed the spider zombie reaper; an intent brief naming the cross-cutting concern ("grep the monorepo") with an executable verify field would have caught it. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7; shipped via direct sage-instruction update in session ed5d3df5. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.

### c-mo1uw15a-4377fc9722b0 [concluded]
> Does the planner have better information than the implementer?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: No. Both read the same codebase. The planner's enumeration of files, consumers, etc. is no more reliable than the implementer's. Presenting planner enumerations as authoritative actively suppresses the implementer's audit instinct. Name the concern and verification method instead — let the implementer discover the actual set. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.
Created by: e2eab8bf-426b-4dd3-9c10-ddae870e8271

### c-mo1uw22y-9129aa0a62e8 [concluded]
> How should planner decisions be tiered and resolved?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Four tiers: (1) Hardcoded — obviously correct, no variance expected; (2) Configurable preference — set once, not actively learned; (3) Pluggable + measurable — system picks winner via outcome data; (4) Pluggable + value-gated — requires patron signal. Most decisions just proceed: Tiers 1–3 are made, recorded, and moved forward. Only Tier 4 surfaces for ratification, and even then the system has already proceeded with a default. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.

Sean's sharpest framing: 'Requiring input should be akin to an incident in other systems, a real failure.' This reframes patron interaction from blocking approval to post-hoc ratification. Status: dropped
Conclusion: No. Specifying seam contracts IS implementation prediction — it contradicts the design's own core principle (specs describe intent, not implementation). The feedback loop only pays off for revisited decisions, and most decisions aren't revisited. In an AI-agent world, the agent's ability to read the codebase IS the seam. The preference model (accumulated patron corrections) is the registry that matters — it records lore about patron decision patterns, not structural seam metadata. Originally envisioned as runtime strategy patterns, then softened to metadata-only, then dropped entirely after Sean asked 'juice worth the squeeze?' Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. ### c-mo1yb8aj-cec786a3eaa8 [concluded]
> Razor for value-laden planner decisions
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Five criteria, derived from mining 35 patron overrides across 38 specs. A planner decision surfaces to the patron if and only if it is: (1) Vocabulary/pattern establishment — new guild terms, categorical distinctions, patterns other code will follow; (2) Human-facing surface — CLI text, error messages, agent personalities, doc phrasing, UX details; (3) Scope boundary — cutlines between commission and follow-up, 'should we also do X?' questions; (4) Shape of persisted or inter-component data — typed vs opaque, required vs optional, configured vs convention, when other components will consume the shape; (5) Component responsibility boundaries — who owns a behavior across engines/tools/apparatuses, when the decision sets a pattern for ownership. Everything else: pick (confidently or arbitrary-with-flag) + record. Uncertainty is not a surfacing signal — it's a planner bug (read more code). Derived in session 2d4de462; see related click on atlas staleness for cluster-C overrides (stale planner knowledge) which are a freshness problem, not a razor-criterion gap.
Created by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c
Resolved by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c
Created: 2026-04-16T20:48:03.259Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T20:48:17.319Z

### c-mo1yb8nf-4dc57032ce48 [concluded]
> Codifiable policy defaults from override analysis
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Three rules, accounting for ~40% of observed overrides (clusters A+B from the override analysis). These are Sean's architectural taste expressed as standing rules, to be codified in the Astrolabe sage-writer instructions. (1) Prefer removal to deprecation — when refactoring, rip out the old path; no deprecation windows unless patron explicitly asks. (2) Prefer fail-loud to silent fallback — throw on missing input; no defaults-when-absent unless the absent case is a legitimate state. (3) Extend the API at the right layer; don't route around it — if the recommendation involves a workaround or 'the anima handles it via prompt,' default to adding the method/tool instead. Next operational step: update sage-writer instructions directly (not a commission). Derived in session 2d4de462.
Created by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c

Resolved: 2026-04-16T20:48:23.829Z

### c-mo1yb8zb-d15093532db1 [concluded]
> Empirical patron-override rate baseline on Astrolabe planner output
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Baseline measurement as of 2026-04-16: ~3.7% override rate (35 overrides / ~945 total decisions across 38 specs). 24 specs (63%) had zero overrides; 14 (37%) had at least one. Hot spots: writ-parentchild-relationships (5), astrolabe-mvp-part-1 (4), parlour-page-for-oculus (4), configurable-rig-templates-via-guild (4). The planner is already correct on the bulk of decisions when a codebase pattern, architecture spec, or clear technical constraint exists. Future work targets the narrow exception path, not bulk quality. Re-measure after razor + codified defaults ship to evaluate effect. Mining done via yq extraction of patron_override fields from specs/*/decisions.yaml in session 2d4de462.

#### c-mo2c33rv-0d22e9372938 [concluded]
> What writ link types are already in use across the guild?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: 100 links in use across 12 distinct types (queried books_clerk_links on 2026-04-17):

  refines       30
  retries       23
  fulfilled_by  21
  depends on     9   ← space-separated
  builds-on      5
  instance_of    3
  enhances       2
  fixes          2
  relates-to     2
  depends-on     1   ← hyphenated (same semantic as "depends on")
  related        1   ← same semantic as "relates-to"
  supersedes     1

Footgun evidence realized in the data:

- Typo drift: "depends on" (9) vs "depends-on" (1) — same semantic, two spellings. String-matching either one misses ~90% of the dependency data. "blocked-by" (the name the MVP click assumes) doesn't appear at all.
- Overlapping synonyms: relates-to/related; refines/builds-on/enhances — taxonomy drift, no canonical form.
- Three separator conventions coexist: space ("depends on"), hyphen ("depends-on", "relates-to", "builds-on"), underscore ("fulfilled_by", "instance_of"). No convention enforcement.

Conclusion: the open-string type field has already produced inconsistent usage at the 100-link scale. Any mechanism that relies on Spider/consumer code string-matching specific type names will have silent misses on existing data unless a canonicalization pass or a reserved-name enforcement mechanism is adopted.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:13:38.779Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:13:47.949Z

#### c-mo2c3bs8-a876bbc50364 [concluded]
> Choose the mechanism for load-bearing writ links (codified open-string vs reserved-name registry vs structural field vs registered-enum)
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Chosen mechanism: the (type, semanticMeaning?) split. Writ links carry a human-readable `type` field (the label, kept as-is from today's schema) and an optional `semanticMeaning` field that references a plugin-owned registered meaning. See child click conclusions for parameter decisions:

- c-mo2db4n7 — split design (label + optional contract reference)
- c-mo2dbdnw — meaning assignment is EXPLICIT (author declares, never inferred)
- c-mo2dbd3d — pairing policy is OPEN (no name-meaning validation)
- c-mo2dbea3 — normalization: lowercase space-delimited canonical form
- c-mo2dbey3 — polarity lives in the meaning schema; names carry no direction metadata

Implementation direction established in session:
- Keep existing `WritLinkDoc.type` field (no rename) — its role shifts from "structural type" to "human-readable label"; normalization applied in place
- Add optional `semanticMeaning?: string` field (id reference) to WritLinkDoc
- Clerk owns the meaning registry; meanings are declared as plugin-manifest kit contributions
- Meaning id format: plugin-qualified with `.` separator (e.g. `spider.precedence-gate`), matching the existing role id convention
- Meaning registry schema is minimal: `{id, ownerPlugin, description}` — direction/cardinality/hooks deferred until a second consumer or cross-plugin coordination creates actual demand

Brief forthcoming for the substrate work (schema change + normalization + registry primitive + migration). Declaring specific meanings (e.g. `spider.precedence-gate`) and wiring Spider to consume them are separate briefs.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:13:49.161Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T04:05:31.661Z

##### c-mo2db4n7-074b97bc036c [concluded]
> Should a writ link's type be a single attribute or split into label + contract?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Split into two attributes on a link:

- `name` — human-readable label, arbitrary string with light normalization. Free-form; expresses what the author wants to say. Consumer code does NOT bind to name.
- `semanticMeaning?` — optional reference (id) into a plugin-owned registry of registered meanings. Each meaning carries direction, cardinality, and policy hooks. Consumer code (e.g. Spider) binds to semanticMeaning.

Key properties of the split:
- Multiple distinct names may map to the same meaning (synonymy is first-class: "depends on", "blocked by", "requires" can all resolve to the same `precedence-gate` meaning).
- Links with meaning=null are documentary only — no policy, no machine behavior.
- Policy/hooks live in the registry entry, referenced by id, so meaning semantics can evolve without rewriting existing links.
- Migration of existing data is zero-loss: current `type` field becomes `name`, meaning starts null, and meanings are assigned later where load-bearing.

Sub-decisions (pairing policy, assignment policy, normalization rules, polarity representation) remain open — see sibling clicks.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:47:52.771Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:48:02.543Z

##### c-mo2dbd3d-83b7ce2cabfc [concluded]
> Should the name↔meaning pairing be a closed list (reject unregistered combos), open with warnings (allow but flag drift), or tiered (hard for polarity/cross-meaning misuse, soft for novel names)?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Open. Any name may be paired with any `semanticMeaning`. No validation or warning on name↔meaning pairings at link creation.

Rationale:
- Closed list (reject non-canonical pairings): drawbacks too high — blocks emergent synonymy for existing meanings, forces ceremony on edge cases, imposes taxonomic decisions up front without empirical data, and creates a migra

Most decisions just proceed: Tiers 1–3 are made, recorded, and moved forward. Only Tier 4 surfaces for ratification, and even then the system has already proceeded with a default. 126	Status: concluded
127	Conclusion: Five criteria, derived from mining 35 patron overrides across 38 specs. A planner decision surfaces to the patron if and only if it is: (1) Vocabulary/pattern establishment — new guild terms, categorical distinctions, patterns other code will follow; (2) Human-facing surface — CLI text, error messages, agent personalities, doc phrasing, UX details; (3) Scope boundary — cutlines between commission and follow-up, 'should we also do X?' questions; (4) Shape of persisted or inter-component data — typed vs opaque, required vs optional, configured vs convention, when other components will consume the shape; (5) Component responsibility boundaries — who owns a behavior across engines/tools/apparatuses, when the decision sets a pattern for ownership. Everything else: pick (confidently or arbitrary-with-flag) + record. Uncertainty is not a surfacing signal — it's a planner bug (read more code). Derived in session 2d4de462; see related click on atlas staleness for cluster-C overrides (stale planner knowledge) which are a freshness problem, not a razor-criterion gap.

400	#### c-mo2c33rv-0d22e9372938 [concluded]
401	> What writ link types are already in use across the guild?
402	Status: concluded
403	Conclusion: 100 links in use across 12 distinct types (queried books_clerk_links on 2026-04-17):
404	
405	  refines       30
406	  retries       23
407	  fulfilled_by  21
408	  depends on     9   ← space-separated
409	  builds-on      5
410	  instance_of    3
411	  enhances       2
412	  fixes          2
413	  relates-to     2
414	  depends-on     1   ← hyphenated (same semantic as "depends on")
415	  related        1   ← same semantic as "relates-to"
416	  supersedes     1
417	
418	Footgun evidence realized in the data:
419	
420	- Typo drift: "depends on" (9) vs "depends-on" (1) — same semantic, two spellings. String-matching either one misses ~90% of the dependency data. "blocked-by" (the name the MVP click assumes) doesn't appear at all.
421	- Overlapping synonyms: relates-to/related; refines/builds-on/enhances — taxonomy drift, no canonical form.
422	- Three separator conventions coexist: space ("depends on"), hyphen ("depends-on", "relates-to", "builds-on"), underscore ("fulfilled_by", "instance_of"). No convention enforcement.
423	
424	Conclusion: the open-string type field has already produced inconsistent usage at the 100-link scale. Any mechanism that relies on Spider/consumer code string-matching specific type names will have silent misses on existing data unless a canonicalization pass or a reserved-name enforcement mechanism is adopted.
425	Created: 2026-04-17T03:13:38.779Z
426	Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:13:47.949Z
427	
428	#### c-mo2c3bs8-a876bbc50364 [concluded]
429	> Choose the mechanism for load-bearing writ links (codified open-string vs reserved-name registry vs structural field vs registered-enum)
430	Status: concluded
431	Conclusion: Chosen mechanism: the (type, semanticMeaning?) split. Writ links carry a human-readable `type` field (the label, kept as-is from today's schema) and an optional `semanticMeaning` field that references a plugin-owned registered meaning. See child click conclusions for parameter decisions:
432	
433	- c-mo2db4n7 — split design (label + optional contract reference)
434	- c-mo2dbdnw — meaning assignment is EXPLICIT (author declares, never inferred)
435	- c-mo2dbd3d — pairing policy is OPEN (no name-meaning validation)
436	- c-mo2dbea3 — normalization: lowercase space-delimited canonical form
437	- c-mo2dbey3 — polarity lives in the meaning schema; names carry no direction metadata
438	
439	Implementation direction established in session:
440	- Keep existing `WritLinkDoc.type` field (no rename) — its role shifts from "structural type" to "human-readable label"; normalization applied in place
441	- Add optional `semanticMeaning?: string` field (id reference) to WritLinkDoc
442	- Clerk owns the meaning registry; meanings are declared as plugin-manifest kit contributions
443	- Meaning id format: plugin-qualified with `.` separator (e.g. `spider.precedence-gate`), matching the existing role id convention
444	- Meaning registry schema is minimal: `{id, ownerPlugin, description}` — direction/cardinality/hooks deferred until a second consumer or cross-plugin coordination creates actual demand
445	
446	Brief forthcoming for the substrate work (schema change + normalization + registry primitive + migration). Declaring specific meanings (e.g. `spider.precedence-gate`) and wiring Spider to consume them are separate briefs.
447	Created: 2026-04-17T03:13:49.161Z
448	Resolved: 2026-04-17T04:05:31.661Z
449	
450	##### c-mo2db4n7-074b97bc036c [concluded]
451	> Should a writ link's type be a single attribute or split into label + contract?
452	Status: concluded
453	Conclusion: Split into two attributes on a link:
454	
455	- `name` — human-readable label, arbitrary string with light normalization. Free-form; expresses what the author wants to say. Consumer code does NOT bind to name.
456	- `semanticMeaning?` — optional reference (id) into a plugin-owned registry of registered meanings. Each meaning carries direction, cardinality, and policy hooks. Consumer code (e.g. Spider) binds to semanticMeaning.
457	
458	Key properties of the split:
459	- Multiple distinct names may map to the same meaning (synonymy is first-class: "depends on", "blocked by", "requires" can all resolve to the same `precedence-gate` meaning).
460	- Links with meaning=null are documentary only — no policy, no machine behavior.
461	- Policy/hooks live in the registry entry, referenced by id, so meaning semantics can evolve without rewriting existing links.
462	- Migration of existing data is zero-loss: current `type` field becomes `name`, meaning starts null, and meanings are assigned later where load-bearing.
463	
464	Sub-decisions (pairing policy, assignment policy, normalization rules, polarity representation) remain open — see sibling clicks.
465	Created: 2026-04-17T03:47:52.771Z
466	Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:48:02.543Z
467	
468	##### c-mo2dbd3d-83b7ce2cabfc [concluded]
469	> Should the name↔meaning pairing be a closed list (reject unregistered combos), open with warnings (allow but flag drift), or tiered (hard for polarity/cross-meaning misuse, soft for novel names)?
470	Status: concluded
471	Conclusion: Open. Any name may be paired with any `semanticMeaning`. No validation or warning on name↔meaning pairings at link creation.
472	
473	Rationale:
474	- Closed list (reject non-canonical pairings): drawbacks too high — blocks emergent synonymy for existing meanings, forces ceremony on edge cases, imposes taxonomic decisions up front without empirical data, and creates a migration tax on novel expression.
475	- Tiered (hard in some cases, soft in others): confusing — different rules per dimension (polarity vs cross-meaning vs novel name) creates a policy surface that authors and reviewers have to hold in their heads.
476	- Warnings-as-logs (allow with a logged warning): not actually load-bearing. Warning channels aren't surfaced to operators in practice, so building the validation infrastructure buys nothing real — the warnings just get swallowed.
477	
478	The open approach accepts one cost: authors can pair nonsensical name+meaning combos (e.g. `{name: "fixes", meaning: precedence-gate}`). Operationally harmless — Spider binds to meaning, not name, so the system behaves correctly. Humans reading the graph may occasionally be confused by a mismatched name/meaning. That confusion is absorbed rather than prevented.
479	
480	Implementation implication: the plugin-owned meaning registry tracks meanings only (direction, cardinality, hooks). No per-meaning canonical-names list is maintained. Name validation is purely normalization (see sibling click on normalization rules). Pairing is never rejected.
481	Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:03.721Z
482	Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:12.400Z
483	
484	##### c-mo2dbdnw-0aa12dfdadec [concluded]
485	> How is semanticMeaning assigned on a link — author-declared explicit, inferred from name, or hybrid?
486	Status: concluded
487	Conclusion: Explicit. The author declares `semanticMeaning` at link creation time; the system never infers it from the name.
488	
489	Rationale: Auto-inference (deriving meaning from a canonical name) creates silent semantic attribution — an author using a name like "depends on" for documentary purposes would have `precedence-gate` meaning attached without intending it, and the Spider would start gating dispatch on a link the author thought was a note. Explicit declaration keeps documentary use documentary and load-bearing use deliberate.
490	
491	Implementation implication: the link-creation API accepts an optional `--meaning` / `semanticMeaning` parameter; omitting it creates a documentary (meaning=null) link. Authors who intend a load-bearing relationship must know the meaning registry and pass the meaning id explicitly. CLI affordances (e.g. `nsg writ link-meanings` to discover registered meanings) are a tooling concern, not part of this decision.
492	
493	Trade accepted: authors can forget to attach meaning and silently produce a documentary link where they intended a load-bearing one. No machine-level protection against that — by design, the system prefers under-attribution to over-attribution.
494	Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:04.460Z
495	Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:04.238Z
496	
497	##### c-mo2dbea3-3c72ffec110b [concluded]
498	> What name normalization rules apply before registry lookup (case, whitespace, separators, synonyms)?
499	Status: concluded
500	Conclusion: Names are normalized to lowercase space-delimited form at link creation. Canonical stored form is the result of applying the following transforms in order:
501	
502	1. Lowercase (e.g. "Depends On" → "depends on")
503	2. Trim leading and trailing whitespace
504	3. Convert camelCase to space-delimited (e.g. "dependsOn" → "depends on")
505	4. Convert snake_case to space-delimited (e.g. "depends_on" → "depends on")
506	5. Convert kebab-case to space-delimited (e.g. "depends-on" → "depends on")
507	6. Collapse multiple internal whitespace characters to a single space
508	
509	Result: "depends on", "depends-on", "depends_on", "dependsOn", "Depends On" all normalize to the canonical form "depends on".
510	
511	Rationale: pick the most natural-language form as canonical. "depends on" dominates in existing data (9 uses vs 1 for "depends-on"), is the most human-readable, and makes normalized names look like prose. Four separator conventions in current use (space / hyphen / underscore / camelCase) all collapse to one.
512	
513	Normalization is purely syntactic — no synonymy mapping. "depends on" and "requires" remain distinct names; if they should resolve to the same behavior, that happens at the meaning layer (both linked to `precedence-gate`), not at normalization.
514	
515	Implementation: normalization applied at insert time and on query. Stored values are always canonical. Querying by name applies the same transform before lookup.
516	Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:05.259Z
517	Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:19.243Z
518	
519	##### c-mo2dbey3-b8e497ebd8c8 [concluded]
520	> How is polarity/direction represented for names that reverse a meaning's canonical direction (e.g., 'blocks' vs 'blocked by')?
521	Status: concluded
522	Conclusion: Polarity lives in the semanticMeaning schema alone. Each registered meaning carries a canonical direction (e.g. precedence-gate: source writ is blocked until target writ completes). Names themselves carry no polarity metadata — consistent with the open pairing policy (names are free labels, not registry entries).
523	
524	Authors control edge direction by choosing which writ is source and which is target, and pick whatever name reads naturally for that direction. If the author wants "A blocks B" semantics (B is gated by A), they create the edge as B→A with any fitting name ("blocks", "blocked by", "requires", etc.). The system interprets based on the meaning's canonical direction; the name is purely human-facing.
525	
526	This dissolves the name-direction mismatch problem (e.g. "blocks" vs "blocked by" reading as the same concept but opposite polarity) — the system simply doesn't look at names for direction; direction is a property of the meaning.
527	Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:06.124Z
528	Resolved: 2026-04-17T04:05:23.158Z
529	
530	#### c-mo2e87dy-172194b5ad53 [concluded]
531	> Declare the spider.precedence-gate meaning — exact description, polarity convention, and the kit contribution that registers it
532	Status: concluded
533	Conclusion: Meaning id: **spider.dependency** (shortened from the working name spider.precedence-gate). Owner: spider plugin.
534	
535	Description: "Source writ cannot dispatch until the target writ reaches `completed`." Gate releases only on `completed` — not on any terminal state. A failed or cancelled blocker does not release the dependent, because the thing the dependent was waiting on was never delivered.
536	
537	Polarity: edge source→target. Source is gated; target is the prerequisite. Names on the link are free-form labels (e.g. "depends on"); the meaning carries the direction.
538	
539	Failure handling: when a blocker reaches `failed` or `cancelled`, Spider transitions the dependent to `stuck` with a stuck-reason naming the blocker writ. Visibility over silence; reversible if the blocker is retried and completes. Chosen over silent-undispatchable (accretes zombie writs) and cascade-fail (destructive and worse for recovery).
540	
541	Declaration packaging: the `supportKit.linkMeanings` entry is added as part of the c-mo2e88aw Spider-wiring commission, not a standalone commission — it's a ~5-line manifest addition with no standalone value.
542	
543	Followup: retry ergonomics spun as a sibling click (see below) — the mechanical recovery path (repost blocker, relink dependent, unlink old) is clunky and is a symptom of the missing retry primitive, not a problem with the gate semantic.
544	Created: 2026-04-17T04:13:35.974Z
545	Resolved: 2026-04-17T13:55:18.436Z
546	
547	#### c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385 [concluded]
548	> Wire Spider to gate dispatch on inbound spider.precedence-gate links — scheduler integration, failed/cancelled-blocker propagation, operator visibility
549	Status: concluded
550	Conclusion: Design fully resolved and dispatched as commission w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc.
551	
552	Final shape: Spider gates dispatch on inbound spider.follows links (conjunctive composition, polling re-evaluation, cycle detection during gate walk, auto-unstick on recovery, provenance via status.spider slot). Two substrate adjustments land alongside: (1) status convention for runtime objects (K8s-style spec/status, plugin-namespaced slots, see c-mo33duvq); (2) link-substrate naming sweep (type→label, semanticMeaning→kind, linkMeanings→linkKinds, separator colon→dot per the system-wide convention at c-mo34644p; see c-mo34jdht). Authoring surfaces (CLI + the existing inline add-link form on the writs page) extended to expose both label and kind. Visibility-gap concern (open status overload) parked separately at c-mo301yp9 for future Oculus work.
553	Created: 2026-04-17T04:13:37.160Z
554	Resolved: 2026-04-17T17:05:43.545Z

556	##### c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3 [concluded]
557	> Lock the spider.follows meaning's final name and description text for the kit contribution
558	Status: concluded
559	Conclusion: Name: **spider.follows**. Owner plugin: spider.
560	
561	Description (verbatim for the kit contribution):
562	
563	"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."
564	
565	Kit-contribution shape:
566	
567	```typescript
568	supportKit: {
569	  linkMeanings: [
570	    {
571	      id: 'spider.follows',
572	      description: 'The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state.',
573	    },
574	  ],
575	},
576	```
577	
578	Rationale for choices:
579	- **`spider.follows`** over `spider.dependency` (implies success-coupling, which the meaning does not), `spider.precedence` (reads direction backwards when paired with source→target convention), or `spider.sequence` (noun for a list, not a relationship). `follows` is a directional verb that reads naturally as source→target ("A follows B" means A comes after B), same 7-char length as `sequence`, unambiguous.
580	- **Terminal-state gate release** with per-consumer policy on individual terminal states — separates the ordering primitive from behavior. Keeps the meaning a pure temporal-ordering contract; Spider's specific stuck-on-failure behavior is its policy, not part of the meaning.
581	
582	This supersedes the stale naming (`spider.dependency`) and description in c-mo2e87dy's conclusion — that click was concluded before these refinements landed.
583	Created: 2026-04-17T14:09:33.558Z
584	Resolved: 2026-04-17T14:09:55.046Z

586	##### c-mo2zimyl-7124983f7459 [concluded]
587	> Should cycles in spider.follows link graphs be prevented by Clerk at link-creation time, or detected by Spider at gate-evaluation time?
588	Status: concluded
589	Conclusion: Spider detects at gate-evaluation time, not Clerk at link-creation. Keep Clerk thin and link-meaning-agnostic. Spider already walks the dependency graph for gate evaluation, so cycle detection is incremental cost — back-edge during the walk. Cycle case surfaces as a stuck dependent with reason 'Cycle detected in spider.follows graph'. Detection latency is bounded by Spider's poll interval; in exchange we avoid building a link-meaning validator hook in Clerk solely for this need.

594	> What mechanism triggers Spider to re-evaluate gates when a blocker's state changes, given the guild has no Clockworks/standing-order substrate yet?
595	Status: concluded
596	Conclusion: Spider's polling loop is the trigger. The same poll cycle that picks up newly-eligible writs re-evaluates gates on currently-gated writs by checking blocker states. No Clockworks/standing-order substrate needed — polling continues to be Spider's main mode of operation, and gate re-evaluation is just one more thing the poll does.

601	> When a retry of a failed blocker eventually completes, does Spider auto-unstick the gated dependent, or does unsticking require explicit operator action?
602	Status: concluded
603	Conclusion: Auto-unstick. When a blocker that previously caused a dependent to be stuck eventually reaches a successful terminal state (e.g., via retry after operator fix), Spider auto-unsticks the gated dependent on the next poll. Rationale: the operator's action *was* fixing the dependency — requiring a second action (manual unstick) is redundant ceremony. The stuck state is purely derived from blocker state; once the blocker is resolved, the stuck condition is gone.

609	Status: concluded

615	> What format should Spider use for the stuck-reason when transitioning a dependent to stuck on blocker failure — free text naming the blocker, structured field, or other?
616	Status: concluded
617	Conclusion: Free text in stuck_reason; the spider.follows link remains the structured source of truth. Use the word 'dependency' (not 'precedence') and reference blockers by short id.
618	
619	Format:
620	- Single blocker:    'Blocked by failed dependency: <short-id>'
621	- Multiple blockers: 'Blocked by failed dependencies: <short-id>, <short-id>'
622	- Cycle case:        'Cycle detected in spider.follows graph'
623

The stuck_reason is a human-readable summary surfacing the specific blocker(s) at a glance. Short ids keep the text readable; full ids remain available via the link itself if needed.

634	> How does Spider identify which stuck writs it owns the stuck-state for, so auto-unstick only fires on Spider-authored stucks?
635	Status: concluded
636	Conclusion: Spider records its stuck transitions in the writ's status.spider slot (per the status convention — see c-mo33duvq). When transitioning a writ to stuck, Spider sets status.spider.stuckCause to one of its values ('failed-blocker', 'cycle'). On poll, auto-unstick logic only considers stuck writs where status.spider?.stuckCause is set. This cleanly separates Spider-authored stucks from stucks caused by other actors. The freeform stuck_reason text remains the human-readable summary (per c-mo2ziqig); status.spider is the machine-actionable provenance.

640	##### c-mo33ejqy-e11ac75d8974 [concluded]
641	> Lifecycle of a gated writ — clarify which writ states are involved in gating (gating happens entirely from 'open', not 'new').
642	Status: concluded
643	Conclusion: Gating happens entirely from 'open'. The 'new' status means draft (author still amending links/content) and is out of scope for Spider gating.
644	
645	Lifecycle:
646	- new (draft): author amending; Spider does not touch
647	- open (gated): finalized; Spider sees on poll, skips dispatch because blockers are non-terminal
648	- open → dispatched: all blockers reached success; Spider assigns a rig
649	- open → stuck: a blocker reached non-success terminal state (Spider sets status.spider.stuckCause = 'failed-blocker' or 'cycle')
650	- stuck → open (auto-unstick): all previously-failed blockers eventually succeeded via retry; Spider clears status.spider.stuckCause and transitions back to open. Next poll picks it up for dispatch if all gates clear.
651	
652	Known visibility gap: 'open' now overloads finalized-and-waiting, finalized-and-eligible, and actively-running. Captured separately at c-mo301yp9 (parked).

750	Status: concluded
751	Conclusion: Patron-override rate is not predicted by any single analysis axis (stakes/category/observable/confidence are all weak discriminators — base rate 6.9%, none above 10%). It is predicted by two orthogonal tests: (1) Reach — the choice must radiate beyond the unit being built; pattern-extending decisions do not need patron review, (2) Patch cost — if the outcome is cheap to reverse via follow-up commission, auto-decide. Plus a third rule: Brief overrides precedent — analyst may not silently recommend against a brief-suggested approach. Applied to sage-analyst.md and sage-reading-analyst.md (committed to /workspace/nexus/). The cosmetic display tags (analysis.category/observable/confidence/stakes) were cut entirely since we surface less now and don't need skim tags; framework-side tag removal dispatched as draft brief w-mo2e38n3-908648e5d420 pending publish.

## c-mo1mq93f-a8d85ce47baf [concluded]
> Redesign Astrolabe planner for intent-and-constraints specs with tiered decision model
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Design closed. The planner redesign resolved into: (1) intent-and-constraints specs (not prescriptive implementation) — concluded children c-mo1uw0ah, c-mo1uw15a; (2) binary pick+record / surface model replacing the originally-proposed 4 tiers — concluded child c-mo1mq9qa (tier model scrapped), c-mo1mq9f6 (preference model dropped as not-MVP); (3) a five-criterion razor for which decisions surface — c-mo1yb8aj; (4) three codified policy defaults that account for ~40% of observed overrides — c-mo1yb8nf; (5) empirical baseline of ~3.7% override rate across 38 specs for future comparison — c-mo1yb8zb; (6) ratification-not-approval as the patron-interaction model — c-mo1uw2ml. Handoff-note design (c-mo1mqa1y) dropped after X010 H1 findings reverted the default engine to single-session implement. Three lines dropped explicitly: seam contracts registry, task type attribute, mandatory terminal verify task. Operational follow-up is draft brief writ w-mo1yp84y-7bf521a80d63 (Update Astrolabe sage instructions with value-laden razor and three codified defaults) covering the four sage roles (reader / reading-analyst / analyst / writer). Review and publish that writ to commission the sage-instruction updates. Shipped infrastructure: v1 task manifest (w-mo0ypqs4) and v2 task-loop engine (w-mo0yqdyr) — the task-loop engine remains opt-in per X010 policy reversion.

### c-mo1mq9f6-ee8e8da74657 [dropped]
> Design the preference model that graduates patron corrections into planning defaults
Status: dropped
Conclusion: Dropped as not-MVP. Override-rate analysis shows the manual learning loop (patron reads records, updates sage instructions) is already operational; automating correction-graduation offers diminishing returns versus simply codifying Sean's already-expressed policy preferences (see sibling click 'Codifiable policy defaults from override analysis'). ### c-mo1mq9qa-6c047858cc63 [concluded]
> Design tiered decision model that proceeds on best-guess defaults
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Tier model scrapped. Replaced by a binary pick+record / surface split with a razor (see sibling click 'Razor for value-laden planner decisions'). The 4-tier framing presumed Tier 1-3 plumbing distinctions would be useful MVP design; empirical override analysis of 35 overrides across 38 specs showed (a) the pick/surface binary is the only edge that matters at MVP, and (b) ~40% of what looked like Tier 4 value-judgments are actually codifiable policy preferences Sean has already expressed, not per-decision taste. Tiered plumbing can be designed later if concrete decisions reveal the need. Decision made in session 2d4de462.

> Should specs prescribe implementation or describe intent and constraints?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Intent and constraints. The planner's job is decision closure and context distillation, not predicting code. Overly prescriptive specs suppress implementer judgment and propagate planner errors faithfully. Validated against w-mny1zoc9 — its prescriptive rename table missed the spider zombie reaper; an intent brief naming the cross-cutting concern ("grep the monorepo") with an executable verify field would have caught it. ### c-mo1uw15a-4377fc9722b0 [concluded]
> Does the planner have better information than the implementer?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: No. Both read the same codebase. The planner's enumeration of files, consumers, etc. is no more reliable than the implementer's. Presenting planner enumerations as authoritative actively suppresses the implementer's audit instinct. Name the concern and verification method instead — let the implementer discover the actual set. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.

### c-mo1uw22y-9129aa0a62e8 [concluded]
> How should planner decisions be tiered and resolved?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Four tiers: (1) Hardcoded — obviously correct, no variance expected; (2) Configurable preference — set once, not actively learned; (3) Pluggable + measurable — system picks winner via outcome data; (4) Pluggable + value-gated — requires patron signal. Most decisions just proceed: Tiers 1–3 are made, recorded, and moved forward. Only Tier 4 surfaces for ratification, and even then the system has already proceeded with a default. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.

### c-mo1uw37b-2d60bc55e32c [dropped]
> Should we maintain a seam contracts / policy registry?
Status: dropped
Conclusion: No. Specifying seam contracts IS implementation prediction — it contradicts the design's own core principle (specs describe intent, not implementation). The feedback loop only pays off for revisited decisions, and most decisions aren't revisited. In an AI-agent world, the agent's ability to read the codebase IS the seam. The preference model (accumulated patron corrections) is the registry that matters — it records lore about patron decision patterns, not structural seam metadata. Originally envisioned as runtime strategy patterns, then softened to metadata-only, then dropped entirely after Sean asked 'juice worth the squeeze?' Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.

### c-mo1yb8aj-cec786a3eaa8 [concluded]
> Razor for value-laden planner decisions
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Five criteria, derived from mining 35 patron overrides across 38 specs. A planner decision surfaces to the patron if and only if it is: (1) Vocabulary/pattern establishment — new guild terms, categorical distinctions, patterns other code will follow; (2) Human-facing surface — CLI text, error messages, agent personalities, doc phrasing, UX details; (3) Scope boundary — cutlines between commission and follow-up, 'should we also do X?' questions; (4) Shape of persisted or inter-component data — typed vs opaque, required vs optional, configured vs convention, when other components will consume the shape; (5) Component responsibility boundaries — who owns a behavior across engines/tools/apparatuses, when the decision sets a pattern for ownership. Everything else: pick (confidently or arbitrary-with-flag) + record. Uncertainty is not a surfacing signal — it's a planner bug (read more code). Derived in session 2d4de462; see related click on atlas staleness for cluster-C overrides (stale planner knowledge) which are a freshness problem, not a razor-criterion gap.
Created by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c

### c-mo1yb8nf-4dc57032ce48 [concluded]
> Codifiable policy defaults from override analysis
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Three rules, accounting for ~40% of observed overrides (clusters A+B from the override analysis). These are Sean's architectural taste expressed as standing rules, to be codified in the Astrolabe sage-writer instructions. (1) Prefer removal to deprecation — when refactoring, rip out the old path; no deprecation windows unless patron explicitly asks. (2) Prefer fail-loud to silent fallback — throw on missing input; no defaults-when-absent unless the absent case is a legitimate state. (3) Extend the API at the right layer; don't route around it — if the recommendation involves a workaround or 'the anima handles it via prompt,' default to adding the method/tool instead. Next operational step: update sage-writer instructions directly (not a commission). Derived in session 2d4de462.

### c-mo1yb8zb-d15093532db1 [concluded]
> Empirical patron-override rate baseline on Astrolabe planner output
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Baseline measurement as of 2026-04-16: ~3.7% override rate (35 overrides / ~945 total decisions across 38 specs). 24 specs (63%) had zero overrides; 14 (37%) had at least one. Hot spots: writ-parentchild-relationships (5), astrolabe-mvp-part-1 (4), parlour-page-for-oculus (4), configurable-rig-templates-via-guild (4). The planner is already correct on the bulk of decisions when a codebase pattern, architecture spec, or clear technical constraint exists. Future work targets the narrow exception path, not bulk quality. Re-measure after razor + codified defaults ship to evaluate effect. Mining done via yq extraction of patron_override fields from specs/*/decisions.yaml in session 2d4de462.

Status: concluded
Conclusion: Reconcile-engine via LLM, with stuck-escalation as fallback. On seal failure, graft a two-engine tail: (1) a quick engine where an anima attempts a rebase of the writ's changes onto the new HEAD, emitting structured success/failure output; (2) a clockwork engine that attempts push-with-ff-only-rebase. Single LLM attempt; if step 2 still can't push, the rig goes stuck. Commissioned in brief w-mo21fy79-c6e76e507be3.

#### c-mo2c33rv-0d22e9372938 [concluded]
> What writ link types are already in use across the guild?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: 100 links in use across 12 distinct types (queried books_clerk_links on 2026-04-17):

  refines       30
  retries       23
  fulfilled_by  21
  depends on     9   ← space-separated
  builds-on      5
  instance_of    3
  enhances       2
  fixes          2
  relates-to     2
  depends-on     1   ← hyphenated (same semantic as "depends on")
  related        1   ← same semantic as "relates-to"
  supersedes     1

Footgun evidence realized in the data:

- Typo drift: "depends on" (9) vs "depends-on" (1) — same semantic, two spellings. String-matching either one misses ~90% of the dependency data. "blocked-by" (the name the MVP click assumes) doesn't appear at all.
- Overlapping synonyms: relates-to/related; refines/builds-on/enhances — taxonomy drift, no canonical form.
- Three separator conventions coexist: space ("depends on"), hyphen ("depends-on", "relates-to", "builds-on"), underscore ("fulfilled_by", "instance_of"). No convention enforcement.

Conclusion: the open-string type field has already produced inconsistent usage at the 100-link scale. Any mechanism that relies on Spider/consumer code string-matching specific type names will have silent misses on existing data unless a canonicalization pass or a reserved-name enforcement mechanism is adopted.

#### c-mo2c3bs8-a876bbc50364 [concluded]
> Choose the mechanism for load-bearing writ links (codified open-string vs reserved-name registry vs structural field vs registered-enum)
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Chosen mechanism: the (type, semanticMeaning?) split. Writ links carry a human-readable `type` field (the label, kept as-is from today's schema) and an optional `semanticMeaning` field that references a plugin-owned registered meaning. See child click conclusions for parameter decisions:

- c-mo2db4n7 — split design (label + optional contract reference)
- c-mo2dbdnw — meaning assignment is EXPLICIT (author declares, never inferred)
- c-mo2dbd3d — pairing policy is OPEN (no name-meaning validation)
- c-mo2dbea3 — normalization: lowercase space-delimited canonical form
- c-mo2dbey3 — polarity lives in the meaning schema; names carry no direction metadata

Implementation direction established in session:
- Keep existing `WritLinkDoc.type` field (no rename) — its role shifts from "structural type" to "human-readable label"; normalization applied in place
- Add optional `semanticMeaning?: string` field (id reference) to WritLinkDoc
- Clerk owns the meaning registry; meanings are declared as plugin-manifest kit contributions
- Meaning id format: plugin-qualified with `.` separator (e.g. `spider.precedence-gate`), matching the existing role id convention
- Meaning registry schema is minimal: `{id, ownerPlugin, description}` — direction/cardinality/hooks deferred until a second consumer or cross-plugin coordination creates actual demand

Brief forthcoming for the substrate work (schema change + normalization + registry primitive + migration). Declaring specific meanings (e.g. `spider.precedence-gate`) and wiring Spider to consume them are separate briefs.

##### c-mo2db4n7-074b97bc036c [concluded]
> Should a writ link's type be a single attribute or split into label + contract?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Split into two attributes on a link:

- `name` — human-readable label, arbitrary string with light normalization. Free-form; expresses what the author wants to say. Consumer code does NOT bind to name.
- `semanticMeaning?` — optional reference (id) into a plugin-owned registry of registered meanings. Each meaning carries direction, cardinality, and policy hooks. Consumer code (e.g. Spider) binds to semanticMeaning.

Key properties of the split:
- Multiple distinct names may map to the same meaning (synonymy is first-class: "depends on", "blocked by", "requires" can all resolve to the same `precedence-gate` meaning).
- Links with meaning=null are documentary only — no policy, no machine behavior.
- Policy/hooks live in the registry entry, referenced by id, so meaning semantics can evolve without rewriting existing links.
- Migration of existing data is zero-loss: current `type` field becomes `name`, meaning starts null, and meanings are assigned later where load-bearing.

Sub-decisions (pairing policy, assignment policy, normalization rules, polarity representation) remain open — see sibling clicks.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:47:52.771Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:48:02.543Z

##### c-mo2dbd3d-83b7ce2cabfc [concluded]
> Should the name↔meaning pairing be a closed list (reject unregistered combos), open with warnings (allow but flag drift), or tiered (hard for polarity/cross-meaning misuse, soft for novel names)?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Open. Any name may be paired with any `semanticMeaning`. No validation or warning on name↔meaning pairings at link creation.

Rationale:
- Closed list (reject non-canonical pairings): drawbacks too high — blocks emergent synonymy for existing meanings, forces ceremony on edge cases, imposes taxonomic decisions up front without empirical data, and creates a migration tax on novel expression.
- Tiered (hard in some cases, soft in others): confusing — different rules per dimension (polarity vs cross-meaning vs novel name) creates a policy surface that authors and reviewers have to hold in their heads.
- Warnings-as-logs (allow with a logged warning): not actually load-bearing. Warning channels aren't surfaced to operators in practice, so building the validation infrastructure buys nothing real — the warnings just get swallowed.

The open approach accepts one cost: authors can pair nonsensical name+meaning combos (e.g. `{name: "fixes", meaning: precedence-gate}`). Operationally harmless — Spider binds to meaning, not name, so the system behaves correctly. Humans reading the graph may occasionally be confused by a mismatched name/meaning. That confusion is absorbed rather than prevented.

Implementation implication: the plugin-owned meaning registry tracks meanings only (direction, cardinality, hooks). No per-meaning canonical-names list is maintained. Name validation is purely normalization (see sibling click on normalization rules). Pairing is never rejected.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:03.721Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:12.400Z

##### c-mo2dbdnw-0aa12dfdadec [concluded]
> How is semanticMeaning assigned on a link — author-declared explicit, inferred from name, or hybrid?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Explicit. The author declares `semanticMeaning` at link creation time; the system never infers it from the name.

Rationale: Auto-inference (deriving meaning from a canonical name) creates silent semantic attribution — an author using a name like "depends on" for documentary purposes would have `precedence-gate` meaning attached without intending it, and the Spider would start gating dispatch on a link the author thought was a note. Explicit declaration keeps documentary use documentary and load-bearing use deliberate.

Implementation implication: the link-creation API accepts an optional `--meaning` / `semanticMeaning` parameter; omitting it creates a documentary (meaning=null) link. Authors who intend a load-bearing relationship must know the meaning registry and pass the meaning id explicitly. CLI affordances (e.g. `nsg writ link-meanings` to discover registered meanings) are a tooling concern, not part of this decision.

Trade accepted: authors can forget to attach meaning and silently produce a documentary link where they intended a load-bearing one. No machine-level protection against that — by design, the system prefers under-attribution to over-attribution.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:04.460Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:04.238Z

##### c-mo2dbea3-3c72ffec110b [concluded]
> What name normalization rules apply before registry lookup (case, whitespace, separators, synonyms)?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Names are normalized to lowercase space-delimited form at link creation. Canonical stored form is the result of applying the following transforms in order:

1. Lowercase (e.g. "Depends On" → "depends on")
2. Trim leading and trailing whitespace
3. Convert camelCase to space-delimited (e.g. "dependsOn" → "depends on")
4. Convert snake_case to space-delimited (e.g. "depends_on" → "depends on")
5. Convert kebab-case to space-delimited (e.g. "depends-on" → "depends on")
6. Collapse multiple internal whitespace characters to a single space

Result: "depends on", "depends-on", "depends_on", "dependsOn", "Depends On" all normalize to the canonical form "depends on".

Rationale: pick the most natural-language form as canonical. "depends on" dominates in existing data (9 uses vs 1 for "depends-on"), is the most human-readable, and makes normalized names look like prose. Four separator conventions in current use (space / hyphen / underscore / camelCase) all collapse to one.

Normalization is purely syntactic — no synonymy mapping. "depends on" and "requires" remain distinct names; if they should resolve to the same behavior, that happens at the meaning layer (both linked to `precedence-gate`), not at normalization.

Implementation: normalization applied at insert time and on query. Stored values are always canonical. Querying by name applies the same transform before lookup.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:05.259Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:19.243Z

##### c-mo2dbey3-b8e497ebd8c8 [concluded]
> How is polarity/direction represented for names that reverse a meaning's canonical direction (e.g., 'blocks' vs 'blocked by')?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Polarity lives in the semanticMeaning schema alone. Each registered meaning carries a canonical direction (e.g. precedence-gate: source writ is blocked until target writ completes). Names themselves carry no polarity metadata — consistent with the open pairing policy (names are free labels, not registry entries).

Authors control edge direction by choosing which writ is source and which is target, and pick whatever name reads naturally for that direction. If the author wants "A blocks B" semantics (B is gated by A), they create the edge as B→A with any fitting name ("blocks", "blocked by", "requires", etc.). The system interprets based on the meaning's canonical direction; the name is purely human-facing.

This dissolves the name-direction mismatch problem (e.g. "blocks" vs "blocked by" reading as the same concept but opposite polarity) — the system simply doesn't look at names for direction; direction is a property of the meaning.

#### c-mo2e87dy-172194b5ad53 [concluded]
> Declare the spider.precedence-gate meaning — exact description, polarity convention, and the kit contribution that registers it
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Meaning id: **spider.dependency** (shortened from the working name spider.precedence-gate). Owner: spider plugin.

Description: "Source writ cannot dispatch until the target writ reaches `completed`." Gate releases only on `completed` — not on any terminal state. A failed or cancelled blocker does not release the dependent, because the thing the dependent was waiting on was never delivered.

Polarity: edge source→target. Source is gated; target is the prerequisite. Names on the link are free-form labels (e.g. "depends on"); the meaning carries the direction.

Failure handling: when a blocker reaches `failed` or `cancelled`, Spider transitions the dependent to `stuck` with a stuck-reason naming the blocker writ. Visibility over silence; reversible if the blocker is retried and completes. Chosen over silent-undispatchable (accretes zombie writs) and cascade-fail (destructive and worse for recovery).

Declaration packaging: the `supportKit.linkMeanings` entry is added as part of the c-mo2e88aw Spider-wiring commission, not a standalone commission — it's a ~5-line manifest addition with no standalone value.

Followup: retry ergonomics spun as a sibling click (see below) — the mechanical recovery path (repost blocker, relink dependent, unlink old) is clunky and is a symptom of the missing retry primitive, not a problem with the gate semantic.
Created: 2026-04-17T04:13:35.974Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T13:55:18.436Z

##### c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3 [concluded]
> Lock the spider.follows meaning's final name and description text for the kit contribution
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Name: **spider.follows**. Owner plugin: spider.

Description (verbatim for the kit contribution):

"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."

Kit-contribution shape:

```typescript
supportKit: {
  linkMeanings: [
    {
      id: 'spider.follows',
      description: 'The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state.',
    },
  ],
},
```

Rationale for choices:
- **`spider.follows`** over `spider.dependency` (implies success-coupling, which the meaning does not), `spider.precedence` (reads direction backwards when paired with source→target convention), or `spider.sequence` (noun for a list, not a relationship). `follows` is a directional verb that reads naturally as source→target ("A follows B" means A comes after B), same 7-char length as `sequence`, unambiguous.
- **Terminal-state gate release** with per-consumer policy on individual terminal states — separates the ordering primitive from behavior. Keeps the meaning a pure temporal-ordering contract; Spider's specific stuck-on-failure behavior is its policy, not part of the meaning.

This supersedes the stale naming (`spider.dependency`) and description in c-mo2e87dy's conclusion — that click was concluded before these refinements landed.

##### c-mo2ziowh-c709c84be3e9 [concluded]
> When a retry of a failed blocker eventually completes, does Spider auto-unstick the gated dependent, or does unsticking require explicit operator action?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Auto-unstick. When a blocker that previously caused a dependent to be stuck eventually reaches a successful terminal state (e.g., via retry after operator fix), Spider auto-unsticks the gated dependent on the next poll. Rationale: the operator's action *was* fixing the dependency — requiring a second action (manual unstick) is redundant ceremony. The stuck state is purely derived from blocker state; once the blocker is resolved, the stuck condition is gone.

##### c-mo33eicl-f8922749cc33 [concluded]
> How does Spider identify which stuck writs it owns the stuck-state for, so auto-unstick only fires on Spider-authored stucks?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Spider records its stuck transitions in the writ's status.spider slot (per the status convention — see c-mo33duvq). When transitioning a writ to stuck, Spider sets status.spider.stuckCause to one of its values ('failed-blocker', 'cycle'). On poll, auto-unstick logic only considers stuck writs where status.spider?.stuckCause is set. This cleanly separates Spider-authored stucks from stucks caused by other actors. The freeform stuck_reason text remains the human-readable summary (per c-mo2ziqig); status.spider is the machine-actionable provenance.

##### c-mo33ejqy-e11ac75d8974 [concluded]
> Lifecycle of a gated writ — clarify which writ states are involved in gating (gating happens entirely from 'open', not 'new').
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Gating happens entirely from 'open'. The 'new' status means draft (author still amending links/content) and is out of scope for Spider gating.

Lifecycle:
- new (draft): author amending; Spider does not touch
- open (gated): finalized; Spider sees on poll, skips dispatch because blockers are non-terminal
- open → dispatched: all blockers reached success; Spider assigns a rig
- open → stuck: a blocker reached non-success terminal state (Spider sets status.spider.stuckCause = 'failed-blocker' or 'cycle')
- stuck → open (auto-unstick): all previously-failed blockers eventually succeeded via retry; Spider clears status.spider.stuckCause and transitions back to open. Next poll picks it up for dispatch if all gates clear.

Known visibility gap: 'open' now overloads finalized-and-waiting, finalized-and-eligible, and actively-running. Captured separately at c-mo301yp9 (parked).
Created: 2026-04-17T15:58:22.330Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T15:58:36.376Z

#### c-mo56pq2k-46e8eb22a103 [concluded]
> Choose retry mechanism for autonomous hopper: Option 1 repost+autoUnstick-extension (follow retries), Option 2 multi-rig-lite (relax 1:1 rig-per-writ, attempts = rigs.length), Option 3 hybrid (multi-rig-lite for automated retry, keep reposting for patron manual retry with brief changes)
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Option 2 (multi-rig-lite): one writ accumulates rig children as attempts; rigs.length is the attempt counter; clockwork transitions writ stuck→open to spawn the next rig. Chosen because it keeps writ identity stable (dependents are gated on the right thing by construction), aligns with the writs-as-obligations / rigs-as-attempts separation, and sidesteps needing autoUnstick to follow retries links — a mechanism Sean explicitly ruled out as hacky. Retries link type stays (experiments consume it to identify failed/invalid writs), but non-load-bearing for scheduling. Rejected Option 1 (per-retry new writ + autoUnstick follows retries) and Option 3 (hybrid) because both require the auto-follow-retries mechanism. Patron edit-and-retry is a separate design question, not a prereq.

lastPrompt: whats the next biggest step for our autonomous operation click

content.text:
# Where the autonomous-operation umbrella stands

Quick orientation before naming the next step.

**Recently landed / in flight under the MVP milestone (`Deliver autonomous-but-serial hopper operation`):**
- ✅ `spider.follows` dependency gating — shipped
- ✅ Combined brief→implementation rig — shipped (tactical bridge ahead of multi-rig)
- 🛠 Retry primitive — Slice A (`w-mo81bdcc`, retryable flag) and Slice B (`w-mo81bdzr`, clockwork requeue, N=2 cap) both **dispatched earlier today**. Waiting on them to land.
- ✅ Stuck-rig detection — turned out to already be covered by the animator heartbeat reconciler; that click just got concluded as a non-gap.

**Still-open MVP-milestone children (design not started):**
1. **Intake tier model** — backlog / ready pool / parked, decoupling dump from dispatch.
2. **Queue observability** — surfacing what's running/queued/stuck/parked (Oculus-side).

## Two readings of "next biggest step"

**Narrow (close the MVP milestone):** the retry commissions are already in flight, so the next design gate is the **intake tier model**. It's the structural piece that's been living as a stub click since day one. Without it the hopper has no real notion of "what to pull next" beyond naive dispatch order — which is tolerable at maxConcurrentEngines:1 but starts biting the moment we want the queue to behave like a queue rather than a pile.

**Wide (the whole umbrella):** the elephant is the **writs-vs-rigs multi-rig refactor** (`c-mo1mqeti`). Massive subtree — 17 live design clicks spanning parent-link untangling, typed workspace artifacts, rig-template composition, brief→mandate collapse, rig-level Clockworks events. Nothing dispatched yet, entirely in design. The serial-hopper MVP was explicitly framed so this refactor *isn't* a prereq, but it's the next major structural chapter once the MVP lands.

## Recommendation

If you want to *close* the MVP milestone:
1. Let the two retry commissions land, then review.
2. Open design on **intake tier model** next — it's the last undesigned MVP-milestone child that's clearly in scope.
3. Queue observability after that (it's smaller and partly Oculus-layer).

If you want to *start the next chapter* while retries bake: pick an entry point into the **writs/rigs multi-rig subtree** — probably the parent-link untangling (`c-mo1mqf54`), since it's the structural root the rest branches from.

Which reading were you asking about, Sean?

content: We're scoping the next step for the autonomous-operation click; retry primitive commissions (w-mo81bdcc, w-mo81bdzr) dispatched today and are in flight. Next action: decide whether to open design on the intake tier model or start the writs-vs-rigs multi-rig refactor. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: do we actually need multi-rig implementation at this point? i can't remmeber why it was considered requied for autonomous operation, perhaps outside of the retry case (Which we handled with multirig-lite)

content.text:
Good challenge — you're not misremembering. Let me walk through the original motivations and see what survived.

## Why multi-rig was originally framed as needed

Four interrelated concerns drove the multi-rig framing:

1. **Retry / multiple attempts per writ** — "if one rig fails, how do we try again?"
2. **Dependency gating across the brief/mandate split** — two writs for one obligation broke `blocked_by` semantics, so you couldn't cleanly gate downstream work on "this commission is done."
3. **Cross-rig data flow** — planning rig produces a plan that an implementation rig consumes, and there's no typed contract for that handoff.
4. **Parent-link overloading** — the `parentId` field carried decomposition, blocking sub-task, and follow-up meanings all at once, making it unreliable as a scheduler primitive.

## What's actually been solved tactically

- **Retry** → multirig-lite (Option 2). One writ, many rigs, `rigs.length` as attempt counter, clockwork-driven `stuck→open`. Dispatched yesterday as Slices A + B. No full refactor needed.
- **Dependency gating** → `spider.follows` plus the combined brief→implementation rig. The combined rig was explicitly framed as a tactical bridge: the brief is the sole writ, no phantom mandate, dependency gating works by construction.
- **Brief→mandate collapse motivator** → subsumed into the combined rig above. The pain point (double-writ breaking `blocked_by`) is gone at MVP scale.
- **Parent-link overloading** → real confusion, but not a capability gate. Current overloading works; it's a cleanliness debt.

## What multi-rig would still uniquely give us

Going through the subtree honestly, the remaining multi-rig payoffs are **feature expansions**, not autonomy gates:

- **Planning-rig as distinct phase** with a three-way outcome (refined / decomposed / rejected) — enables richer planning flows, but we're fine collapsing today.
- **Typed workspace artifacts** — enables real pipelines where rig B consumes rig A's structured output. Not needed while a commission is one rig.
- **Rig-level Clockworks events** (`rig.spawned`, `rig.completed`) — finer-grained policy hooks; writ-level events are sufficient for the hopper.
- **Rig template composition** (initial / recovery / continuation) — would be nice for varied rig shapes, but multirig-lite already gives us "another rig of the same shape" for retry.
- **Seal-engine workspace conflict** (session container vs bare clone) — this one I'd flag as possibly still load-bearing, worth double-checking. If the seal engine has been running fine, it's resolved in practice; if it's brittle, multi-rig's typed workspace model would properly fix it.

## My read

You're right. Multi-rig should be **demoted from autonomous-operation prereq to enabling refactor for future capabilities**. Nothing in the remaining MVP milestone children (intake tier model, queue observability) depends on it. The structural wins from the refactor are real but they pay off in *what comes next* — richer planning, artifact pipelines, parallel operation — not in crossing the autonomous-serial finish line.

The one thing worth sanity-checking before fully demoting: has the seal-engine workspace conflict caused any operational grief recently? If not, I'd say the refactor is genuinely optional for autonomy.

Want me to capture this as a conclusion on the multi-rig parent click — reframe it as "deferred, not blocking MVP autonomy" — and note the seal-workspace question as the one to verify?

2	description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
3	---
4	
5	# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow
6	
7	> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.
8	
9	A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.
10	
11	Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:
12	
13	- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
14	- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
15	- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
16	- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
17	- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.
18	
19	## When to open a click
20	
21	Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:
22	
23	- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
24	- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
25	- A decision is deferred pending more thought
26	- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion
27	
28	Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.
29	
30	## Creating a click
31	
32	    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]
33	
34	- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
35	- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
36	- The click starts in `live` status.
37	
38	### Vocabulary discovery habit
39	
40	When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.
41	
42	## Viewing clicks
43	
44	Two commands do the orientation work. Reach for them in this order:
45	
46	**1. Tree view** — board-level scan, the first thing you run at startup:
47	
48	    nsg click tree [--root-id <id>] [--status live] [--depth N]
49	
50	Renders the click forest with box-drawing connectors and status indicators:
51	```
52	● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
53	  ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
54	  ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
55	  └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
56	```
57	Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.
58	
59	**2. Extract a subtree** — the primary narrative-loading command, used whenever the conversation turns toward a specific area:
60	
61	    nsg click extract --id <id> [--format md|json]
62	
63	One call loads the whole subtree — goals, statuses, and conclusions for any concluded/dropped clicks — as a structured document. This is how you orient on any line of inquiry.
64	
65	**Don't do this:** don't walk a subtree by calling `show` on each child to reconstruct the narrative. That's what `extract` is for. If you find yourself running `show` three or more times in a row to understand the shape of an area, stop and run `extract` on the common ancestor instead.
66	
67	**Show a single click** — use only for single-click inspection (checking one click's links, parent, status, or conclusion):
68	
69	    nsg click show <id>
70	
71	**List with filters:**
72	
73	    nsg click list [--status live] [--status parked] [--limit 50]
74	
75	## Status transitions
76	
77	```
78	live ──> parked       (park — deliberately dormant, pick up later)
79	parked ──> live       (resume — back to active exploration)
80	live ──> concluded    (conclude — decision reached, conclusion required)
81	live ──> dropped      (drop — abandoned, reason required)
82	parked ──> concluded  (conclude directly from parked)
83	parked ──> dropped    (drop directly from parked)
84	```
85	
86	Terminal states (concluded, dropped) are immutable — no further changes allowed.
87	
88	**Park** a click (live -> parked):
89	
90	    nsg click park --id <id>
91	
92	**Resume** a parked click (parked -> live):
93	
94	    nsg click resume --id <id>
95	
96	**Conclude** a click with a decision:
97	
98	    nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "We decided to..."
99	
100	**Drop** a click without a decision:
101	
102	    nsg click drop --id <id> --conclusion "Moot because..."
103	
104	### Conclusions are tight
105	
106	A conclusion is a pointer-note for future-me, not an essay for posterity. Target shape: **decision + one-line reasoning + (optional) one-clause naming the rejected alternatives.** A small paragraph, not a screenful.
107	
108	- **No markdown structure.** No headers, no tables, no "Recommendation:" sections. If a conclusion needs structure, it's too long.
109	- **Don't re-argue rejected options.** Name them in a clause ("rejected `spider.sequence` for directional ambiguity"); don't rebuild the tradeoff table.
110	- **Exploration stays in the session transcript.** The analytical work — enumerated cases, stress tests, option matrices — belongs in chat, joinable via session ID. Only the distilled outcome lands in the conclusion.
111	
112	Verbose conclusions are the main context cost of clicks. Keep them short and the tree can grow freely.
113	
114	## Sub-questions (children)
115	
116	When a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks:
117	
118	    nsg click create --goal "Sub-question A?" --parent-id <parent-id>
119	
120	Children are the decomposition mechanism. Resolving a parent typically means resolving or dropping its children. But there's no automatic cascading — a parent can be concluded while children are still live (e.g., "we answered the main question; remaining sub-questions are moot").
121	
122	### Enumerated decisions → enumerated children
123	
124	When the analysis inside a click naturally enumerates N items with per-item evaluations (N sources → include/exclude each, N options → pick-one across them, N candidates → accept/reject each), those evaluations are latent sub-clicks. Lift them into children rather than collapsing them into the parent's conclusion.
125	
126	**Recognition trigger:** if you catch yourself writing a conclusion that contains a per-item list with individual rationale ("source A: yes because X; source B: no because Y; …"), stop — that list is telling you the decisions want their own clicks. The parent's conclusion should name the *principle* or *summary*; each child's conclusion carries one item's rationale.
127	
128	This aligns with conclusion-discipline (keeps each conclusion tight, no inline tables) and with capture-liberally (the tree is cheap; per-item decisions are durable structural references worth having).
129	
130	## Links
131	
132	Typed links connect clicks to each other or to writs (cross-substrate):
133	
134	    nsg click link --source-id <click-id> --target-id <target-id> --link-type <type>
135	
136	Link types:
137	- `related` — lateral cross-reference
138	- `commissioned` — this click's conclusion produced a commission (target is a writ ID)
139	- `supersedes` — this click replaces the target
140	- `depends-on` — this click can't be concluded until the target is resolved
141	
142	**Commissioned links are automatic.** When a click's conclusion spawns a writ (brief posted, commission dispatched), create the `commissioned` link from the click to the writ id without asking. This preserves the click-to-artifact trail in the tree and is cheap to maintain. If multiple clicks contribute to one commission, link the narrowest conclusion-bearing click; add additional links from contributing parents only if the trail would be lossy otherwise.
143	
144	Remove a link:
145	
146	    nsg click unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>
147	
148	## Reparenting
149	
150	Move a click to a new parent or to root:
151	
152	    nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent-id <new-parent-id>
153	    nsg click reparent --id <id>                               # move to root
154	
155	Circular parentage is detected and rejected.
156	
157	## Short ID support
158	
159	All ID parameters accept short prefixes (e.g., `c-mo1ee` instead of the full ID). The Ratchet resolves prefixes and errors on ambiguity.
160	
161	## Clicks and session continuity
162	
163	Clicks provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:
164	
165	- **Startup** — scan the click tree with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` to see what's in flight. When the conversation turns toward a specific area, use `nsg click extract --id <id>` to load the full subtree as narrative context. Reserve `nsg click show <id>` for single-click inspection. Don't eagerly read every click.
166	- **During the session** — open new clicks for substantial inquiries; create child clicks for sub-questions. Conclude or drop clicks as decisions are reached.
167	- **Wrap-up** — park any live clicks that won't be continued immediately. Conclude clicks where decisions were reached this session. The click tree itself is the session's durable record of what was explored and decided.
168	
169	Because click goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once, there's no body to maintain or keep in sync. The session transcript (joinable via session ID) holds the full exploration context. Clicks are just the structural skeleton — questions asked and answers given.
